5 min read
Just now

--

Part 1 of 3
Managers are already making decisions on ground that has been quietly reshaped by Artificial Intelligence, yet most of our academic theories still treat the machine as a sidekick rather than as part of the terrain.
The hidden layer beneath managerial judgment
For several decades, managerial decision making has been framed as a dual accomplishment of intuition and cognition: fast, pattern‑based judgment alongside slow, analytic reasoning. AI has arrived not as a third, neatly separable “decider,” but as a pervasive substrate that filters data, frames problems and surfaces cues before either intuition or cognition is engaged.
In practice, this means that AI increasingly determines which options enter a manager’s choice set, how risks and opportunities are represented, and which signals feel salient enough for intuition to latch onto. When theory positions AI merely as an adviser sitting beside the human, it misses these upstream effects.
Consequently, they miss some of the most important ways AI reshapes managerial work.
Press enter or click to view image in full size

Where is AI positioned in decision-making?A field that studies only one half of judgment
My systematic review makes visible an imbalance: the empirical corpus on AI in managerial decision making overwhelmingly studies the cognitive half of the dual‑process model and largely neglects the intuitive half. There is detailed work on efficiency, optimisation, predictive accuracy, and model performance, but almost no sustained examination of when and how managers use intuition to accept, revise, or override AI outputs.
This imbalance matters because it is precisely the intuitive faculty that governs what managers do when an AI recommendation conflicts with their experience, their sense of timing, or the “feel” of a context. Intuition appears in passing as an acknowledged but unstudied partner, while the cognitive faculty is extensively quantified. The result is a literature well equipped to say when AI improves accuracy, but poorly equipped to guide managers when they suspect the machine is misleading them.
A tale of 2 problems: Why location and definition both mislead us
Two linked problems run through the existing work I synthesise: where AI is placed in the decision process, and how AI is defined.
First, location. Most studies position AI either alongside the manager as an external adviser or against the manager as a rival decider whose recommendations can be accepted or rejected. On that view, the main question becomes whether the human should follow the recommendation at the moment of choice, a framing that steers empirical attention to cognitive evaluation and leaves the substrate-like shaping of the problem space under-examined.
Second, definition. Much of the corpus inherits its vocabulary from computer science and software engineering, defining AI by architecture — machine learning, deep learning, neural networks, or large language models. Managers, however, engage what the system does and how it presents its outputs, not the internals of gradient boosting versus transformers, so architectural definitions are conceptually misaligned with decision research.
This combination, AI mislocated at the point of choice and defined in terms of its inner machinery, encourages the field to treat AI as a discrete technical object rather than as a functional participant in decision processes. My review argues that a more productive move is to define AI by its functional role in the decision, and to relocate it from “late-stage adviser” to “early-stage substrate.”
Why a systematic review, and why now
The timing of my review is not incidental. Organisations have adopted AI at near‑ubiquitous scale, yet the proportion of firms that realise material value remains modest, indicating that the adoption problem is in part a decision problem. AI is increasingly argued to differ from conventional information technology not only by processing more data, but by actively reshaping the cognitive task of the manager, altering what must be judged rather than merely aiding judgment.
At the same time, evidence relevant to this intersection is fragmented across disciplines — information systems, organisational behaviour, strategy, human resources, and psychology — with cognitive and intuitive streams often advancing separately and AI bolted on as an external input. My systematic review responds by assembling and screening 4,572 records down to a final corpus of 67 studies, catalogued against an approximately 30‑field extraction scheme, making it possible to trace patterns across architecture, function, theory, task type, and decision context.

The three gaps that demand a new map
Reading across the corpus, my review surfaces three interlocking gaps that justify re‑mapping AI-aided managerial decision making.Report8V2.pdf+1

• Intuitive blind spot. The field gives extensive attention to cognitive optimisation and only cursory attention to the intuitive faculty that governs how and when managers override or reframe AI advice.

• Technical definitions without functional typologies. Most studies define AI architecturally and rarely classify aids by what they actually do in the decision, leaving decision researchers without a typology tied to managerial interaction.

• Atheoretical engagement with decision-making. A substantial subset of studies proceed without invoking any decision-making theory, and even when theory is used, it tends to come from organisational or classical traditions rather than from the cognitive–intuitive lineage most relevant to human–AI interaction.
Together, these gaps mean that the literature cannot yet explain how AI changes the interplay of intuition and cognition, nor specify the conditions under which managers should trust, contest, or re‑design their relationship with the machine. A “map” that relocates AI as a substrate and classifies it by function is, in this sense, not an optional refinement but a necessary repair.

What this means for managers and organisations
For managers, the substrate framing changes the questions that matter. Instead of asking only “Should I follow this AI recommendation?”, the more consequential question becomes
“How has this AI already shaped the problem I am seeing, the options I am considering, and the cues my intuition is reading?”
Decision quality, on this view, rests not only on evaluating visible outputs but on understanding the machinery behind the framing layer.
For organisations, the review suggests that investing in AI without revisiting decision authority, training, and theory is likely to reproduce the adoption–value gap. If AI silently narrows choice sets or embeds certain heuristics in dashboards and workflows, then governance must contend with substrate effects: who understands them, who can challenge them, and how intuition is protected rather than eroded under sustained exposure to machine‑conditioned representations.
